title: The Second Sajjhāy of the Nandī Sūtra (Sthavirāvalī)
summary: This is a hymn of homage that recites, in order, the great teachers (sthaviras / ācāryas) who received and handed down the Jain scriptures generation after generation, beginning with Mahāvīra's chief disciple Sudharmā. It is chanted during the fortnightly Pakkhī pratikramaṇa as sacred study (sajjhāy / svādhyāya), honouring the unbroken lineage that preserved the canon. It closes by naming the five kinds of knowledge (jñāna) that the Nandī Sūtra goes on to expound, so the worshipper bows to the teachers and then turns to the teaching they protected.
ritualPlacement: It belongs to the Pākṣika (Pakkhī) pratikramaṇa, recited in the closing study-portion as the second of the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys — immediately after the first (Saṅgha-stuti) sajjhāy and before the Nine Smaraṇas. A sajjhāy is study-recitation (svādhyāya), not one of the six Āvaśyakas. Because the Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī pratikramaṇas carry no separate text of their own and simply follow the Pakkhī order — substituting the word 'pakkhī' with 'cāumāsī' / 'saṁvatsarī' and lengthening the kāyotsarga — this sajjhāy recurs in them unchanged. Stated once here; the per-verse commentaries rely on this and will not repeat it.
authorAndSource: These gāthās are extracted from the Nandī Sūtra, a Śvetāmbara Mūla / Cūlikā-sūtra traditionally attributed to Devavācaka (Devarddhigaṇi Kṣamāśramaṇa), redactor of the canon at the Valabhī recension (c. 5th–6th century CE). They form the sūtra's opening Sthavirāvalī (paṭṭāvalī, the succession-roll of teachers). The half-line in v.27, 'therāvalīyā sammattā' ('the Sthavirāvalī is concluded'), explicitly marks the close of the lineage section and the start of the five-jñāna enumeration.
structureArc: vv.1–2 open the homage with the earliest succession after Mahāvīra — Sudharmā (Agnivaiśyāyana gotra), Jambū (Kāśyapa), Prabhava (Kātyāyana), Śayyambhava, Yaśobhadra, Sambhūta, Bhadrabāhu, Sthūlabhadra and Gautama. vv.3–8 continue the line through teachers named by gotra — Mahāgiri, Suhastī, Śrīvata, the Kauśika- and Hārita-gotra ācāryas, Ārya Samudra, Ārya Maṅgu, Ārya Dharma, Bhadragupta, Ārya Vajra (Vajrasvāmī) and Ārya Rakṣita. vv.9–11 praise Nandī, the sādhvī Lakṣmaṇā and the vācaka-vaṁśa (lineage of scripture-reciters) of Nāgahastī and Revatī-nakṣatra. vv.12–23 honour the later guardians of the Kālika-śruta anuyoga — Skandila, Himavanta, Nāgārjuna, Govinda, Bhūtadinna, Lohitya and Dūṣyagaṇi. vv.24–25 offer collective homage to the yuga-pradhāna teachers and their 'soft-soled feet'. v.26 bows to all the remaining masters of the Kālika anuyoga and announces the poet will now expound knowledge ('nāṇassa parūvaṇaṁ vucchaṁ'). v.27 declares the Sthavirāvalī concluded and lists the five jñānas — abhinibodhika (mati), śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyaya and kevala — the Nandī's principal subject.
recurringImagery: The whole hymn is built on a single repeated act: vande / vandāmi — 'I bow'. Each teacher is placed by his gotra (clan-lineage) and adorned with stock similes that recur from verse to verse: the ocean unshaken and fathomless (Ārya Samudra, 'akkhubhiya-samudda-gambhīra'), the Himālaya for steadfastness and endurance (Himavanta), fine gold and the spotless lotus for moral purity, and a jewel-casket (rayaṇa-karaṇḍaga) that keeps the anuyoga safe (Ārya Rakṣita). The governing motif is the vācaka-vaṁśa / paṭṭa-paramparā — the unbroken 'reader-lineage' through which the oral canon (kālika-śruta anuyoga) was guarded and transmitted. Note: the famous Saṅgha-as-city / chariot / lotus / ocean imagery belongs to the FIRST Nandī sajjhāy (the Saṅgha-stuti); this second sajjhāy is the teacher-lineage, so its imagery is lineage-devotion, not the saṅgha-nagara. Explained once here; each verse simply applies the homage-and-simile pattern to the teacher it names.
keyThemes: Transmission and preservation of the kālika-śruta
Steadfastness, purity and depth of the ācāryas

glossary.definition: An elder; a senior monk-teacher who holds and transmits the tradition.
The roll or chain of elders — the ordered succession of teachers, named verse by verse in this sajjhāy.
A pontifical succession-list recording who held the teacher's seat (paṭṭa) after whom.
A 'reader/reciter' — an ācārya authorized to expound the scriptures; hence vācaka-vaṁśa, the lineage of such expounders.
Systematic exposition or commentary of a scriptural text; here especially of the Kālika-śruta.
The class of canonical texts studied only at fixed (kālika) times; the body of teaching the named masters preserved.
Clan or family-line; teachers are identified by gotra (e.g. Kāśyapa, Kauśika, Hārita).
A monastic group or school descending from a particular teacher.
Sacred self-study and recitation of scripture; the ritual category to which this sajjhāy belongs (not one of the six Āvaśyakas).
Reverent salutation; the repeated act (vande / vandāmi) that structures every verse.
The pre-eminent teacher of an age, the foremost authority of his generation.
'Forbearing ascetic', an honorific title of revered teachers (e.g. Himavanta Khamāsamaṇa).
Knowledge; the Nandī's central subject, classed as five — mati (abhinibodhika), śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyaya and kevala.
'Jewel-casket' — the simile for a teacher who keeps the anuyoga safe, as a casket guards gems (applied to Ārya Rakṣita).

howToRead: Newcomers can read it simply as a roll-call of gratitude — you needn't recognize every name; just feel the unbroken chain of teachers stretching from Mahāvīra's disciple Sudharmā down to the canon's redactor. Advanced practitioners can track the gotra markers and the shift from the early paṭṭāvalī (vv.1–8) to the vācaka-vaṁśa and Kālika-anuyoga masters (vv.9–25), culminating in the five-jñāna enumeration (v.27) that the Nandī Sūtra proper develops.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)
sources.consultedFor: Ritual placement of the two Nandī sajjhāys within the Pakkhī study-portion and the cāumāsī/saṁvatsarī substitution rule.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary — Nandī Sūtra (Nandīsūtra)
sources.consultedFor: Nandī Sūtra as a Śvetāmbara Mūla/Cūlikā-sūtra, its Sthavirāvalī, and the five-jñāna classification.

notes: Teacher names are normalized to IAST in this preface, while the recited gāthās preserve their Prakrit forms (e.g. Suhamma = Sudharmā, Jambū = Jambūsvāmī, Vaïra = Vajra/Vajrasvāmī, Khandila = Skandila); follow the book verbatim for every mātrā, anusvāra and conjunct, and do not normalize the recited text. Several teachers are identified only by gotra, and a few exact identifications/dates are traditional rather than firmly historical — treat individual name-equations as the tradition's, flagging any uncertainty per verse. The first source's URL is an internal corpus path, not a public web link. The Saṅgha-as-city/chariot/lotus/ocean imagery noted elsewhere in the corpus belongs to the FIRST Nandī sajjhāy (Saṅgha-stuti), not to this lineage hymn.
